articles.title: Scientists found a bug that generates its own heat in freezing cold
articles.summary: Snow flies have an unexpected way of surviving freezing temperatures. They produce antifreeze proteins to block ice formation and can even generate their own heat. Scientists also found that their genes are unusually unique, and they feel less cold-related pain than other insects. These combined traits let them stay active in conditions that would freeze most species.
articles.content:
Snow flies might seem like ordinary insects, but their survival strategy is anything but typical.

In a new study, scientists at Northwestern University investigated how these small, wingless insects, which move across snowy surfaces to find mates and lay eggs, stay alive in freezing conditions. They discovered that snow flies rely on a surprising mix of biological tools. The insects can generate their own body heat like mammals and produce antifreeze proteins similar to those found in Arctic fish.

While most insects cannot survive below freezing, snow flies remain active at temperatures as low as -6 degrees Celsius (or 21.2 degrees Fahrenheit).

These findings provide new insight into how life adapts to extreme environments. They may also help researchers develop new ways to protect cells, tissues and materials from damage caused by cold.

The study was published on March 24 in the journal Current Biology.

"Insects are cold-blooded, so they are at the mercy of external temperatures," said Northwestern's Marco Gallio, who led the study. "But they have a mind-boggling ability to adapt to extremes. When it gets cold, a common strategy is to find shelter and become dormant until conditions get better. But instead of slowing down, snow flies actually prefer freezing cold, snowy conditions and hide away when the snow melts and it gets warm. They really push the limit of what's possible. Now we've found snow flies aren't just tolerating the cold, they have multiple ways to counteract it."

Gallio studies how temperature shapes biology and is the Soretta and Henry Shapiro Research Professor in Molecular Biology as well as a professor of neurobiology at Northwestern's Weinberg College of Arts and Sciences. He co-led the study with Marcus Stensmyr, a biology professor at Lund University in Sweden. Other Northwestern contributors include William Kath of the McCormick School of Engineering and Alessia Para from Weinberg. Gallio and Kath are also affiliated with the NSF-Simons National Institute for Theory and Mathematics in Biology (NITMB).

Unusual Genes and Antifreeze Proteins

To understand how snow flies survive such harsh conditions, researchers first examined their genetic makeup. Gallio and his team were the first to sequence the snow fly genome and compare it with related insects that are not adapted to cold environments. They also analyzed RNA to identify which genes are actively used for survival in freezing temperatures. These complex comparisons were carried out by Richard Suhendra, a Ph.D. student working with Kath.

The results were unexpected.

"We couldn't find many of the genes within any database," Gallio said. "Initially, I thought we must have sequenced some alien species. It's very rare for an active gene, which makes a protein, to not have a match."

Further investigation showed that these unusual genes produce antifreeze proteins. Like those found in Arctic fish, these proteins attach to ice crystals and prevent them from growing. This process protects cells from damage during freezing.

"Remarkably, some of the antifreeze proteins we found are actually structurally related to those of Arctic fish," Gallio said. "That suggests evolution came to the same solution for a common problem."

Heat Production Helps Snow Flies Stay Active

The team also identified genes linked to energy use and cellular processes involved in producing heat. This suggested another unexpected ability. Snow flies do not just resist freezing, they also generate their own heat.

"We found genes that in larger animals are associated with mitochondrial thermogenesis in brown adipose tissue," Gallio said. "Many animals like marmots and polar bears have brown fat, which is there to produce heat. When they go into hibernation, they burn this stored fat to produce heat rather than to produce chemical energy. So, in some ways snow flies use a combination of the strategies used by polar bears and by Arctic fish."

Blocking Ice and Creating Warmth

To test how the antifreeze proteins work, Matthew Capek, a Ph.D. student in the Gallio Lab, modified fruit flies to produce one of the snow fly proteins. He then exposed them to freezing temperatures in a lab freezer. The modified flies survived at much higher rates than normal fruit flies, confirming that the proteins act as barriers that stop ice from spreading.

In another experiment, researchers tested whether snow flies actually generate heat. They measured the insects' internal temperature while gradually lowering the surrounding temperature below freezing. During this process, snow flies consistently remained slightly warmer than expected by a couple of degrees Celsius compared to other insects.

"Other insects, like bees and moths, shiver to increase their heat," Stensmyr said. "But we found no evidence of shivering. Snow flies instead likely produce heat at the cellular level, more similar to how mammals and even some plants generate heat."

Even a small increase in temperature can be critical for survival in such extreme conditions. This brief warmth may give snow flies enough time to find shelter and avoid freezing when temperatures suddenly drop.

Reduced Sensitivity to Cold Pain

Snow flies also appear to be less sensitive to the painful effects of extreme cold. Most people recognize the sharp sting of touching ice or cold metal. This sensation is triggered by reactive molecules in cells that signal the body to avoid harm. In snow flies, this response is significantly reduced.

Gallio and his team found that a key sensory protein involved in detecting harmful stimuli is much less responsive in snow flies than in other insects. As a result, these insects can tolerate higher levels of cold-related stress and continue functioning in conditions that would overwhelm most species.

"It turns out that a specific irritant receptor is 30 times less sensitive in snow flies than in mosquitoes and fruit flies," Gallio said. "So, they can cope with higher levels of noxious irritants produced by cold exposure."

Future Research on Extreme Cold Survival

Next, the researchers plan to explore in greater detail how snow flies generate heat at the cellular level and to identify the full range of antifreeze proteins they produce. This work could reveal whether other organisms use similar strategies to survive in extreme cold environments.

The study, "Coordinated molecular and physiological adaptations enable activity at subfreezing temperature in the snow fly Chionea alexandriana," will appear in the April 6 volume of the journal Current Biology and feature on the cover. The work in the various labs was partially supported by the National Institutes of Health, the Pew Scholars Program, the McKnight Foundation, the Paula M. Trienens Institute for Sustainability and Energy, the Crafoord Foundation, the National Science Foundation, the Simons Foundation and NITMB. External collaborators included the DNAzoo project and Olga Dudchenko and Erez Lieberman Aiden, who are both faculty members at Rice University and at the Baylor College of Medicine.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about snow flies' unique biological adaptations to survive freezing temperatures, including heat generation and antifreeze proteins. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding life in extreme environments and potential applications in protecting cells and materials from cold damage, benefiting society at large. The article is focused, detailed, and highlights a novel, impactful scientific advancement.
articles.personality_title: Snow flies survive freezing cold by making their own heat and antifreeze
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Snow flies are small insects that live in snowy places where temperatures often fall below freezing. Most insects cannot survive such cold, but snow flies stay active even when it is very cold.

**What happened** – Scientists at Northwestern University studied snow flies to understand how they survive freezing weather. They found that snow flies produce special antifreeze proteins that stop ice from forming inside their bodies. They also discovered that snow flies generate their own heat, a rare ability for insects. The team sequenced the snow fly’s genes and found many unique ones that help with surviving cold. Experiments showed that these proteins protect cells from freezing and that snow flies keep their body temperature a few degrees warmer than the cold air around them.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a new way that life can adapt to extreme cold. Snow flies use a combination of strategies seen in fish and mammals to survive. Understanding these proteins and heat-making processes could help scientists find new ways to protect living cells and materials from cold damage, which might be useful in medicine and technology.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study exactly how snow flies produce heat inside their cells and to learn more about the different antifreeze proteins they make. This could reveal if other animals or plants use similar methods to survive cold.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Snow flies survive freezing temperatures by producing antifreeze proteins and their own heat, revealing new ways animals adapt to extreme cold.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les mouches des neiges survivent au froid glacial en produisant leur propre chaleur et antigel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mouches des neiges sont de petits insectes qui vivent dans des zones enneigées où la température descend souvent en dessous de zéro. La plupart des insectes ne peuvent pas survivre à un froid aussi intense, mais les mouches des neiges restent actives malgré cela.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université Northwestern ont étudié ces mouches pour comprendre comment elles survivent au froid glacial. Ils ont découvert que ces insectes produisent des protéines antigel spéciales qui empêchent la formation de glace dans leur corps. Ils ont aussi trouvé que les mouches des neiges génèrent leur propre chaleur, ce qui est rare chez les insectes. En analysant leurs gènes, ils ont identifié de nombreux gènes uniques qui aident à survivre au froid. Des expériences ont montré que ces protéines protègent les cellules du gel et que les mouches maintiennent leur température corporelle un peu plus élevée que l’air froid.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une nouvelle manière dont la vie peut s’adapter à un froid extrême. Les mouches des neiges utilisent une combinaison de stratégies observées chez les poissons et les mammifères. Comprendre ces protéines antigel et la production de chaleur pourrait aider à protéger les cellules et les matériaux du froid, ce qui serait utile en médecine et en technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier précisément comment les mouches produisent de la chaleur dans leurs cellules et découvrir plus de protéines antigel qu’elles fabriquent. Cela pourrait montrer si d’autres animaux ou plantes utilisent des méthodes similaires pour survivre au froid.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les mouches des neiges survivent au froid glacial en produisant des protéines antigel et leur propre chaleur, révélant de nouvelles façons dont les animaux s’adaptent au froid extrême.
articles.personality_title_es: Las moscas de la nieve sobreviven al frío extremo produciendo su propio calor y anticongelante
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las moscas de la nieve son insectos pequeños que viven en lugares con nieve donde las temperaturas suelen estar bajo cero. La mayoría de los insectos no pueden sobrevivir a este frío, pero las moscas de la nieve permanecen activas incluso en temperaturas muy bajas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Northwestern estudiaron a las moscas de la nieve para entender cómo sobreviven al frío extremo. Descubrieron que producen proteínas anticongelantes que evitan que se forme hielo dentro de sus cuerpos. También encontraron que estas moscas generan su propio calor, algo raro en insectos. Al analizar sus genes, hallaron muchos genes únicos que ayudan a sobrevivir al frío. Experimentos mostraron que estas proteínas protegen las células del congelamiento y que las moscas mantienen su temperatura corporal unos grados más alta que el aire frío.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra una nueva forma en que la vida se adapta al frío extremo. Las moscas usan una combinación de estrategias vistas en peces y mamíferos para sobrevivir. Entender estas proteínas y la producción de calor podría ayudar a proteger células y materiales del frío, con posibles aplicaciones en medicina y tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar cómo generan calor dentro de sus células y conocer más proteínas anticongelantes que producen. Esto podría mostrar si otros organismos usan métodos similares para sobrevivir al frío.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las moscas de la nieve sobreviven a temperaturas bajo cero produciendo proteínas anticongelantes y su propio calor, mostrando nuevas formas de adaptación al frío extremo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warmly colored painting of a small, wingless snow fly resting on a snow-covered branch, subtly glowing with a soft internal warmth that gently melts the surrounding ice crystals, while delicate, translucent protein-like structures shimmer around it, symbolizing its unique antifreeze and heat-generating abilities in a serene, cold winter landscape.

articles.title: Computer finds flaw in major physics paper for first time
articles.summary: A computer language designed to robustly verify mathematical theorems and expose logical flaws has been turned towards a physics paper – and spotted an error. The discovery raises questions about how many other papers may harbour similar issues
articles.content:
A computer language designed to robustly verify mathematical theorems and expose logical flaws has been turned towards a physics paper – and spotted an error. The discovery raises questions about how many other papers may harbour similar issues

Machines can help spot mathematical errors Alamy Stock Photo

A computer language created to spot errors in mathematical theorems has uncovered a fundamental error in a widely cited physics paper for the first time. The researcher behind the discovery says it is the first physics paper he has analysed in this way, which raises a worrying question: how many more contain mistakes?

Specialised software is increasingly used to help mathematicians check their proofs are correct and free of contradictions and logical holes, using a process known as formalisation. The approach has even been proffered as a potential solution to some of the thorniest problems in mathematics, such as Shinichi Mochizuki’s sprawling, 500-page proof for the ABC conjecture, which experts have quibbled over for years.

Now, Joseph Tooby-Smith at the University of Bath, UK, has turned a formalisation language called Lean towards the field of physics. He attempted to formalise research published in 2006 on the stability of the two Higgs doublet model (2HDM) potential, which has been widely cited in the years since, but accidentally revealed an error that undermines the theorem.

Formalised theorems can be used as building blocks to formalise more complex theorems, and Tooby-Smith says that his work was supposed to be a “tick box exercise” to add the paper to a larger project of formalised physics research called PhysLib, modelled on an established database for mathematics called MathsLib. “We’re not going out there to disprove papers; we’re going out there to build results that everyone can use,” says Tooby-Smith.

The error relates to a statement in which the original authors say that a certain condition, C, is sufficient for a stable solution to the problem. But Tooby-Smith showed during formalisation that there is a condition C that doesn’t provide a stable solution.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Tooby-Smith says that the discovery of the error has a dramatic effect on the paper, but is unlikely to cause problems downstream in work that has built on it and cited it. However, he now fears that many physics papers harbour similar mistakes, but isn’t certain how wide-ranging the problem might be. He thinks this makes a strong case for formalisation to become a standard part of publishing new research.

Tooby-Smith says that physicists tend not to give as much explicit detail in theorems as mathematicians. “Because a lot of physicists aren’t interested in these nitty-gritty details, sometimes they miss them, and that’s where you get an error,” he says.

Kevin Buzzard at Imperial College London says that formalisation is having a big impact on mathematics, and that there is no reason that theoretical physics, at least, can’t be treated in the same way. “We tried to do maths like this, and it turned out to be really interesting,” he says.

But the real benefit of formalisation in maths is now coming from the large corpus of existing formalised theorems, which allows human mathematicians to more readily build on top of them and also to train AI models that can help formalise new theorems faster. Training those AI models to formalise mathematics took time and lots of concrete examples to use as training data, which might not yet be available for physics.

“Ideally, we need a million lines of physics, and that might be hard work to get. If the machines aren’t pretty good at doing physics initially, then there’ll be manual work at the beginning, and then eventually the machines will hopefully take over,” says Buzzard.

The authors of the original physics paper didn’t respond to a request for comment from New Scientist, but Tooby-Smith says that he informed them of his discovery, received confirmation that they agreed and was told that an erratum would be published.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough where a computer language designed to verify mathematical theorems has uncovered a fundamental error in a widely cited physics paper, highlighting the potential for formal verification to improve scientific accuracy and reliability. This has broad implications for the scientific community and could lead to more rigorous standards in research publication, benefiting society by enhancing trust and progress in science.
articles.personality_title: Computer language finds important error in famous physics paper
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often rely on complex math in physics papers to explain how the universe works. Recently, special computer programs have been created to check math proofs carefully for mistakes.

**What happened** – Joseph Tooby-Smith from the University of Bath used a computer language called Lean, designed to check math theorems, to analyze a well-known physics paper from 2006 about a model called the two Higgs doublet model. During this process, the computer found a mistake in the paper’s main claim about stability conditions.

**Impact** – This is the first time such software has found an error in a physics paper, showing that even important and often-cited research can have hidden problems. It suggests many physics papers might have similar mistakes because physicists sometimes skip detailed proofs unlike mathematicians. This discovery highlights the value of using formal computer checks to improve accuracy in physics research.

**What’s next step** – Tooby-Smith plans to continue formalizing physics research to build a reliable database called PhysLib. Experts suggest that formal verification could become a standard step in publishing new physics studies. However, training computers to check physics fully will take time because there isn’t yet enough formalized physics data for machines to learn from.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A computer language designed for math has uncovered a key error in a major physics paper, pointing to a new way to improve the reliability of scientific research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un langage informatique découvre une erreur importante dans un article majeur de physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent souvent des mathématiques complexes dans les articles de physique pour expliquer le fonctionnement de l’univers. Récemment, des programmes informatiques spéciaux ont été créés pour vérifier attentivement les preuves mathématiques afin de détecter des erreurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Joseph Tooby-Smith de l’Université de Bath a utilisé un langage informatique appelé Lean, conçu pour vérifier les théorèmes mathématiques, afin d’analyser un article de physique bien connu de 2006 sur un modèle appelé le modèle à deux doubles de Higgs. Pendant ce processus, l’ordinateur a trouvé une erreur dans la principale affirmation de l’article concernant les conditions de stabilité.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un tel logiciel découvre une erreur dans un article de physique, montrant que même des recherches importantes et souvent citées peuvent contenir des problèmes cachés. Cela suggère que de nombreux articles de physique pourraient avoir des erreurs similaires car les physiciens sautent parfois les preuves détaillées contrairement aux mathématiciens. Cette découverte souligne l’importance d’utiliser des vérifications formelles par ordinateur pour améliorer la précision dans la recherche en physique.

**Prochaine étape** – Tooby-Smith prévoit de continuer à formaliser la recherche en physique pour construire une base de données fiable appelée PhysLib. Des experts suggèrent que la vérification formelle pourrait devenir une étape standard dans la publication des nouvelles études en physique. Cependant, entraîner les ordinateurs à vérifier pleinement la physique prendra du temps car il n’y a pas encore assez de données formalisées pour que les machines apprennent.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un langage informatique conçu pour les mathématiques a découvert une erreur clé dans un article majeur de physique, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour améliorer la fiabilité de la recherche scientifique.
articles.personality_title_es: Un lenguaje informático encuentra un error importante en un famoso artículo de física
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos a menudo usan matemáticas complejas en los artículos de física para explicar cómo funciona el universo. Recientemente, se han creado programas informáticos especiales para revisar cuidadosamente las pruebas matemáticas y detectar errores.

**Qué pasó** – Joseph Tooby-Smith de la Universidad de Bath usó un lenguaje informático llamado Lean, diseñado para verificar teoremas matemáticos, para analizar un conocido artículo de física de 2006 sobre un modelo llamado el modelo de dos dobles de Higgs. Durante este proceso, la computadora encontró un error en la afirmación principal del artículo sobre las condiciones de estabilidad.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un software así encuentra un error en un artículo de física, mostrando que incluso investigaciones importantes y muy citadas pueden tener problemas ocultos. Esto sugiere que muchos artículos de física podrían tener errores similares porque los físicos a veces omiten pruebas detalladas, a diferencia de los matemáticos. Este hallazgo destaca el valor de usar verificaciones formales por computadora para mejorar la precisión en la investigación física.

**Próximo paso** – Tooby-Smith planea continuar formalizando la investigación en física para crear una base de datos confiable llamada PhysLib. Expertos sugieren que la verificación formal podría convertirse en un paso estándar en la publicación de nuevos estudios de física. Sin embargo, entrenar a las computadoras para revisar completamente la física tomará tiempo porque aún no hay suficientes datos formalizados para que las máquinas aprendan.

**Frase clave** – Un lenguaje informático diseñado para matemáticas ha descubierto un error clave en un artículo importante de física, señalando una nueva manera de mejorar la confiabilidad de la investigación científica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an ancient, intricate mechanical clockwork interwoven with glowing mathematical symbols and physics formulas, where a gentle, luminous computer chip softly illuminates a subtle flaw in the gears, set against a calm, warm background of soft earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: Temperature gets a new definition using a quantum device
articles.summary: A device that relies on quantum effects and oversized atoms may be a more reliable way to measure temperature that doesn't require calibration
articles.content:
A device that relies on quantum effects and oversized atoms may be a more reliable way to measure temperature that doesn't require calibration

The main part of the new set-up for cooling and trapping rubidium atoms Tomasz Kawalec CC BY-SA 4.0

A better, more reliable definition of temperature could come from a quantum device full of giant atoms.

While some countries measure temperatures in Celsius and others use Fahrenheit, physicists everywhere use a unit called kelvin. Zero kelvin denotes the absolute coldest temperature allowed by the known laws of physics, so kelvin is said to measure “absolute temperature”. In practice, however, making sure that when you measure a single kelvin, it really is a single kelvin is a laborious process.

“If you want to make an absolute temperature measurement, you buy a commercial temperature sensor, which was calibrated by another commercial temperature sensor, which was calibrated by another commercial temperature sensor, and so on. And one of those sensors was, at some point, sent to the National Institute of Standards [and Technology],” says Noah Schlossberger at NIST in Colorado.

He and his colleagues have now built a device that uses quantum effects to measure kelvin, which researchers could use instead of having to get someone else to calibrate their sensors.

The device is a small box made from metal and glass containing trapped rubidium atoms. The researchers push these atoms to an extreme size, using lasers to move the outermost electrons unusually far from the nucleus, and to an extreme temperature by using both lasers and electromagnetic fields to grab onto and chill the atoms to about half a millikelvin, a temperature roughly a 600,00th that of room temperature.

As a result, the outermost electrons in the rubidium atoms become extremely sensitive to even a small increase in temperature and “jump” into a different quantum state when exposed to one. These jumps are what makes the device a great temperature sensor, because there are well-established mathematical models that can determine the temperature differences needed to make them – effectively allowing for a redefinition of kelvin in these terms.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

The International Bureau of Weights and Measures defines the kelvin in a similar way – as a product of several quantum constants – but, in practice, even institutions like NIST use non-quantum devices for calibration. The hope is that the new device gives a quantum definition of kelvin where calibration wouldn’t be needed.

“Every rubidium atom in the world is exactly the same, and they will behave in exactly the same way in the same environment. I can rebuild the device on the other side of the world, and it will be exactly the same,” says Schlossberger. He says this is especially important for keeping high-precision devices functioning correctly, such as atomic clocks, which can only work at very low-kelvin temperatures.

But the new device is still a prototype and so it still has imperfections in how quantum states are detected, for example. It is also too bulky to leave the lab and took more than six months to build. The researchers are now working on optimising its design to make it more practical and to increase its accuracy.

Schlossberger presented the work on 16 March at the American Physical Society Global Physics Summit in Colorado.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough: a quantum device that can measure temperature (kelvin) more reliably without the need for calibration. This innovation has broad implications for scientific measurement standards and precision technologies like atomic clocks, impacting many fields and institutions globally. The article provides detailed context on the device's operation, significance, and potential future improvements, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Quantum device offers a new way to measure temperature without calibration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Temperature is usually measured in kelvin by comparing sensors that need calibration. This process can be long and complicated because every sensor relies on another sensor to check its accuracy.

**What happened** – Scientists at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) built a device using rubidium atoms made very large and very cold with lasers. These atoms change their quantum state when the temperature changes, allowing the device to measure temperature directly through quantum effects.

**Impact** – This device could redefine how we measure kelvin, making temperature measurements more reliable and universal. Since every rubidium atom behaves the same way, the device can be rebuilt anywhere with the same results. This is especially important for precise technologies like atomic clocks that need very exact temperature control.

**What's next step** – The current device is a prototype that is large and takes a long time to build. Researchers will work on making it smaller, easier to use, and more accurate so it can be used outside the lab in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new quantum device using rubidium atoms could measure temperature more accurately and without the need for sensor calibration, improving scientific standards worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un dispositif quantique offre une nouvelle façon de mesurer la température sans étalonnage
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La température est généralement mesurée en kelvin en comparant des capteurs qui nécessitent un étalonnage. Ce processus peut être long et compliqué car chaque capteur dépend d’un autre pour vérifier sa précision.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Institut national des normes et de la technologie (NIST) ont construit un appareil utilisant des atomes de rubidium rendus très grands et très froids grâce à des lasers. Ces atomes changent d’état quantique lorsque la température varie, permettant ainsi à l’appareil de mesurer la température directement grâce aux effets quantiques.

**Impact** – Cet appareil pourrait redéfinir la manière dont nous mesurons le kelvin, rendant les mesures de température plus fiables et universelles. Puisque chaque atome de rubidium se comporte de la même façon, l’appareil peut être reproduit partout avec les mêmes résultats. C’est particulièrement important pour des technologies précises comme les horloges atomiques qui ont besoin d’un contrôle très exact de la température.

**Prochaine étape** – L’appareil actuel est un prototype, volumineux et long à construire. Les chercheurs vont travailler à le rendre plus petit, plus facile à utiliser et plus précis pour qu’il puisse être utilisé hors laboratoire à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau dispositif quantique utilisant des atomes de rubidium pourrait mesurer la température plus précisément et sans besoin d’étalonnage, améliorant les standards scientifiques dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Dispositivo cuántico ofrece una nueva forma de medir la temperatura sin calibración
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La temperatura se mide generalmente en kelvin usando sensores que necesitan calibración. Este proceso puede ser largo y complicado porque cada sensor depende de otro para verificar su precisión.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Instituto Nacional de Estándares y Tecnología (NIST) construyeron un dispositivo que usa átomos de rubidio hechos muy grandes y muy fríos con láseres. Estos átomos cambian su estado cuántico cuando la temperatura cambia, permitiendo medir la temperatura directamente con efectos cuánticos.

**Impacto** – Este dispositivo podría redefinir cómo medimos el kelvin, haciendo las mediciones de temperatura más confiables y universales. Como cada átomo de rubidio se comporta igual, el dispositivo puede replicarse en cualquier lugar con los mismos resultados. Esto es especialmente importante para tecnologías precisas como los relojes atómicos que necesitan un control exacto de la temperatura.

**Próximo paso** – El dispositivo actual es un prototipo grande y que tarda mucho en construirse. Los investigadores trabajarán para hacerlo más pequeño, fácil de usar y más preciso para que pueda usarse fuera del laboratorio en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo dispositivo cuántico que usa átomos de rubidio podría medir la temperatura con mayor precisión y sin necesidad de calibración, mejorando los estándares científicos en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate glass and metal chamber softly glowing from within, containing oversized, gently floating rubidium atoms with extended, glowing electron orbits depicted as luminous rings; subtle beams of laser light and faint electromagnetic waves surround the chamber, symbolizing precise quantum manipulation and the redefinition of temperature at an ultra-cold scale.

articles.title: Scientists create clear nail polish that lets you use touchscreens with long nails
articles.summary: Using a smartphone with long nails can be frustrating, forcing people to awkwardly tap with their fingertips instead of their nails. Now, researchers are working on a clear nail polish that could change that by turning fingernails into touchscreen-friendly tools. By experimenting with dozens of formulas, they discovered that combining common compounds like taurine and ethanolamine can help nails carry just enough electrical charge for screens to detect a touch.
articles.content:
Anyone who has tried to use a smartphone or tablet with long nails knows it takes some adjustment. Instead of tapping naturally with your fingertips, you often have to angle your fingers awkwardly just to make contact with the screen. What if you could simply use your nails instead? Researchers are now working on a clear nail polish that could make that possible by turning long fingernails into touchscreen-compatible styluses.

A team from Centenary College of Louisiana plans to present its findings at the spring meeting of the American Chemical Society (ACS). ACS Spring 2026 features nearly 11,000 presentations across a wide range of scientific fields.

How the Idea Came to Life

The project began when Manasi Desai, an undergraduate student interested in cosmetic chemistry, approached her research advisor Joshua Lawrence in search of a project. Lawrence, an organometallic chemist, says "chemists are here to solve problems and to try to make your world better." They started looking for an everyday issue that chemistry might help address.

They soon noticed how difficult it can be for people with long nails to use smartphones, including a phlebotomist they encountered during a bloodwork appointment. When they asked whether a solution would be helpful, the response was enthusiastic: "yes, please!" That moment inspired Desai's research direction.

Why Touchscreens Do Not Work With Nails

Most modern devices rely on capacitive touchscreens. These screens generate a small electric field across their surface. When a conductive material, such as a fingertip or even a drop of water, interacts with that field, it changes the screen's capacitance. The device detects that change and interprets it as a touch.

However, nonconductive materials like fingernails or pencil erasers do not alter the electric field, so the screen does not respond. For nails to work, they must be able to carry a small electrical charge.

Moving Away From Dark and Hazardous Additives

Earlier attempts to solve this problem involved adding conductive materials such as carbon nanotubes or metallic particles to nail polish. While these approaches worked, they raised safety concerns because the materials can be dangerous if inhaled during manufacturing. They also produced dark or metallic finishes, limiting the range of cosmetic options.

Desai and Lawrence aimed to create a polish that remains clear while also being safe for both users and manufacturers.

Testing Ingredients for Clear Conductive Polish

To find a formula that balanced clarity and conductivity, Desai tested many combinations through trial and error. She experimented with 13 commercially available clear coats and more than 50 additives. Over time, she identified two promising ingredients: forms of taurine, an organic compound commonly sold as a dietary supplement, and ethanolamine, another simple organic molecule.

Ethanolamine provided the desired conductivity and worked well within the polish, but it has some toxicity concerns. Modified taurine is nontoxic but creates a slightly cloudy appearance. When used together, these ingredients produced a formula that allowed a smartphone to register a fingernail touch, marking an important early success.

"Our final, clear polish could be put over any manicure or even bare nails, which could help people with calluses on their fingertips, too. So, it has both a cosmetic and lifestyle benefit," explains Desai.

A Different Chemical Mechanism

Unlike earlier approaches that relied on inherently conductive materials, the researchers believe their formula works through acid-base chemistry. This idea came from the strong performance of ethanolamine-based mixtures, which can release protons that help move electrical charge.

They propose that when the polish interacts with a touchscreen's electric field, these protons move between molecules. This creates a small change in capacitance, just enough for the device to detect a touch.

Promising Results With More Work Ahead

Although the results so far are encouraging, the polish is not ready for commercial use. Even the best-performing ethanolamine-taurine formula does not yet work consistently when applied to nails. In addition, ethanolamine evaporates quickly, so the polish only remains effective for a few hours after application. The team also hopes to replace it with a fully nontoxic alternative.

Despite these challenges, the researchers now better understand how the formula works and continue to test new compounds to improve performance.

"We're doing the hard work of finding things that don't work, and eventually, if you do that long enough, you find something that does," says Lawrence.

The research was funded by the Centenary College of Louisiana, the Albert Sklar Family and the Sklar Chair in Chemistry. The team has also submitted a provisional patent for this work.

Title

Modification of nail polish formulations for conductivity to operate capacitive touchscreens

Abstract

Most smartphones use capacitive touchscreen technology, the operation of which relies on skin conductivity. This is challenging for users with long manicures or zombie fingers. We describe nail polish formulations that achieve sufficient conductivity to disrupt the stored electrical field in capacitive touchscreens and register as touch events. Commercial nail polishes were used to test additives for formula compatibility and electrical performance. Nail polish formulations were coated onto a silicone mat and the resistance of the dried films were measured; formulations with noninfinite resistance were tested on capacitive touchscreens. We will present four successful and numerous unsuccessful formulations.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a scientific innovation with clear real-world benefits, addressing a common usability problem for people with long nails by creating a safe, clear conductive nail polish that enables touchscreen use. The research is detailed, focused, and has broad potential impact for many smartphone users, improving accessibility and convenience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop clear nail polish that works on touchscreens
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – People with long fingernails often find it hard to use smartphones or tablets because nails do not conduct electricity, so touchscreens don’t respond to nail taps. 

**What happened** – A research team from Centenary College of Louisiana created a clear nail polish that lets fingernails carry enough electrical charge to work on capacitive touchscreens. The polish uses safe ingredients like taurine and ethanolamine to make nails touchscreen-friendly. The researchers tested many mixtures to find one that is clear, safe, and effective.

**Impact** – This new polish could help people with long nails use their devices more easily without awkward finger movements. It also avoids dark or harmful additives used in earlier attempts, making it safer and cosmetically appealing. The polish might also help people with fingertip calluses.

**What's next step** – The polish is still being improved to last longer and be fully nontoxic. The team continues testing new formulas and hopes to make it ready for everyday use. They have also applied for a patent to protect their invention.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have created a clear and safer nail polish that allows long nails to work on touchscreens, making device use easier for many people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques développent un vernis transparent qui fonctionne sur les écrans tactiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les personnes avec de longs ongles ont souvent du mal à utiliser les smartphones ou tablettes car les ongles ne conduisent pas l’électricité, donc les écrans tactiles ne réagissent pas aux tapotements des ongles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs du Centenary College de Louisiane a créé un vernis transparent qui permet aux ongles de porter suffisamment de charge électrique pour fonctionner sur les écrans tactiles capacitifs. Le vernis utilise des ingrédients sûrs comme la taurine et l’éthanolamine pour rendre les ongles compatibles avec les écrans.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau vernis pourrait aider les personnes avec de longs ongles à utiliser leurs appareils plus facilement, sans gestes maladroits. Il évite aussi les additifs sombres ou dangereux des tentatives précédentes, ce qui le rend plus sûr et esthétique. Le vernis pourrait aussi aider ceux qui ont des cors au bout des doigts.

**Prochaine étape** – Le vernis est encore en cours d’amélioration pour durer plus longtemps et être totalement non toxique. L’équipe continue à tester de nouvelles formules et espère le rendre prêt pour un usage quotidien. Ils ont aussi déposé un brevet pour protéger leur invention.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont créé un vernis transparent et plus sûr qui permet aux longs ongles de fonctionner sur les écrans tactiles, facilitant ainsi l’utilisation des appareils pour beaucoup de personnes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan un esmalte transparente que funciona en pantallas táctiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las personas con uñas largas a menudo tienen dificultades para usar teléfonos inteligentes o tabletas porque las uñas no conducen electricidad, por lo que las pantallas táctiles no responden a los toques con las uñas.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores del Centenary College de Louisiana creó un esmalte transparente que permite que las uñas lleven suficiente carga eléctrica para funcionar en pantallas táctiles capacitivas. El esmalte usa ingredientes seguros como taurina y etanolamina para hacer que las uñas sean compatibles con las pantallas.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo esmalte podría ayudar a las personas con uñas largas a usar sus dispositivos más fácilmente, sin movimientos incómodos. También evita aditivos oscuros o peligrosos usados en intentos anteriores, haciéndolo más seguro y estético. El esmalte también podría ayudar a personas con callos en las puntas de los dedos.

**Próximo paso** – El esmalte aún se está mejorando para durar más tiempo y ser completamente no tóxico. El equipo continúa probando nuevas fórmulas y espera que esté listo para el uso diario. También han solicitado una patente para proteger su invento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Investigadores han creado un esmalte transparente y más seguro que permite que uñas largas funcionen en pantallas táctiles, facilitando el uso de dispositivos para muchas personas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate hand silhouette with long, translucent fingernails coated in a clear, glossy polish gently touching a softly glowing smartphone screen, with subtle electric currents symbolized by faint, flowing lines connecting the nails to the screen, set against a simple neutral background.
